transcript.text: Thank you.
Hi, Matthew.
How are you?
Just fine.
And yourself?
I'm fine.
Thank you.
Wonderful.
Wonderful.
Pleased to meet you.
My name is Matthew.
I'm the Chief Content Officer here at GrowthX.
Thank

I deal a lot with content quality and client deliverable quality, and then also with our internal tool Atlas to improve its capabilities and work with the team that works with the tool to help improve their capabilities, etc.
So content officer here is a little more internal facing than it is at a lot of companies where it's largely about like external marketing or something like that.
But some external work as well, though, it creates some content that gets published externally.
But pleased to meet you and happy to hear a little bit about you.
Thank you.
All right.
Thank you for having me today.
So I'm a content and marketing person.
I have over nine years of experience, mainly with leading SEO-driven content across multiple countries, especially in the Middle East region, KSA, UAE, Qatar, the GCC mainly.
Most recently in my role at Fathom, which is sort of like a recruitment platform.
Yeah, so I'm managing the blog there.
And of course, as of recently, not very recently, I've been heavily using AI to sort of streamline the process.
Where I grew by the end of like this year, by 86%, I grew the organic traffic to the blog through the assistance of AI.
And yeah, this is mainly what I do in my current role.
I graduated with a BA in translation, English-Arabic.
My Arabic is horrible, but, you know, this is why I went into marketing.
Well, mine's no better, I'm sorry to say.
Yeah, so ever since I graduated, I never like went into translation or anything like that, straight into marketing, writing.
Got it.
Yeah.
Let's
Yeah, that's great.
Yeah, we have some other folks here in our content org that also worked in translation, Spanish to English and Portuguese to English, kind of those realms.
But it's interesting how often translation and, you know, content and language go together for obvious reasons.
Okay, great.
I'd love to talk to you about a handful of things.
So the role that we have here is very process oriented.
And obviously, it does have to do with content.
So we expect anybody coming in to have kind of like a hip pocket understanding of what good content feels like and looks like.
And, you know, you obviously have the experience there.
But the kind of new aspects or still in flux aspects of like this kind of role is obviously using AI tools and LLM tools.
So I'd love to hear a little bit about it, like practical examples are preferred, not just philosophy.
it's a bit that
But like how you're using AI tooling, what tools you use, what capacity you use them in to generate content or to edit content.
All right.
So basically, I use ChatGPT.
That's like my best bud at the moment.
Yeah.
So what I do is I'm currently still in the process of trying to perfect a prompt that could basically cut time in terms of producing and posting blogs.
I'm struggling with Arabic English so far.
Like it's been going great.
Struggling with the Arabic, you know, translation aspect.
not very accurate.
But so I've used it for also other things in terms of workflow within the department other than just producing blogs.
For example, I had a problem where I was facing editing delays because multiple editors were reviewing the same draft.
So there was very slow turnaround.
So I mapped the workflow.
I found duplicate reviews, and I also introduced using AI, like a tailored editing system, sort of.
Also, I started using Grammarly and SEO check tools just to make sure that everyone abides by the checklist.
The checklist I created with the help of AI, of course.
So now everyone just has to run through the checklist.
Tools are there.
They're using the same tools.
Everything is very streamlined, and that reduced the turnaround time for us.
Yeah, and basically, this is mainly it.
But of course, if you want to go into the blog aspect, I, first of all, define the objective of the piece that I'm writing.
Give that to ChatGPT.
I feel like it's a very evolved baby where you feed it, and then it gives you something amazing.
So the more I feed it, yeah, the more I feed it, the better results I get.
So.
So tone, SEO optimization, a bunch of keywords, give it brand guidelines as well.
And I also give it a checklist that I also provide for ChatGPT.

And then it just comes up with the content and then it goes through the cycle of editing, fact checking, still having a bit of trouble with like when it gives you figures or numbers or quotes can be pretty inaccurate.
So I go in and manually check those, edit those.
Most of the time I have to totally edit them.
Yeah, this is mainly like the process that I go through currently.
Gotcha.
And when you were, when you're using like ChatGPT to, to create a process, are you using like custom GPTs or do you work within an individual chat?
Like what's the functional way that you use it since that's your like primary tool?
So I start a project on ChatGPT and basically research like any content related prompts, build on them.
From like good sources, and I build on them, see what works, log what doesn't, log what does, add to it, maybe like remove some stuff from it, try to just like optimize the prompts in general.
Okay, yeah, makes sense.
And then what is your, what, as you kind of went through that, you realize, okay, cool, I need to create this process and, and work through this.
What, what was the kind of the thing that you, in addition to the quirks of ChatTPT, what are the things that you kind of picked up or learned about working with LLMs or, or AI?
Yeah, so what I learned is that maybe you have to be very accurate in terms of what you give it or what you expect of it.
So I learned that, you know, you can't just say, for example, like, it gives me a piece, and I can't just say, make it better, or like, yeah, I need this to be more conversational.
No, no, I have to be very, like, in depth with, like, instructions and what I want, and what I want to achieve also, not just, like, just.
You know, the instructions, I also need to give it the results that I need from this piece.
So, yeah.
Yeah, makes sense.
Examples of good or examples of good outcomes, I think, are often useful.
Yeah.
When you judge a content, obviously, whether you're working with humans or LLMs, this applies to both scenarios, but how do you personally judge when a piece of content is high quality?
Like, what's your personal kind of rubric?
How do you look through that?
Talk to me a little bit about your process there.

So, I always think that any piece of content, like I've been writing for nine years, even before, like, I had the amazing assistance of these tools, is basically for it to be very value-driven, accurate, engaging.
I feel like intent is super important, especially when I'm in the world of SEO.
So, I feel like the intent needs to be clear, structure needs to be clear, and, like,
My main goal currently in my role is for us, like our main concept or what we want to achieve is to build trust and like, of course, performance.
I like every couple of days I go in and track performance, Google Analytics, heat maps, all the good things, just to make sure that it's performing well.
And like, I really like to see where like users are dropping or where like there's a, like there's an issue with content and work to refine that as well.
Yeah.
And then how do you know, I mean, in the world of client delivery, I think it's important to have a strong opinion about good enough.
So talk to me about like your, your good enough moment, like everything can always be better, right?
So how do you kind of pull the cord when you think something, hey, this is good enough.
Talk to me about your, your decision making there.
All right.
So like, to me, to like add on the point that I mentioned beforehand, I feel like,

It's good enough when I, usually what I do, like this is just me, I go in in the place of like the audience or like the user who wants to like see this, get information from it, whether like the intent was transactional, navigational, or informational.
So I put myself in that position and like I'm a mom, so I do a lot of Googling or like asking ChatGPT.

And it's very important, like I get super frustrated when I'm like looking for something or looking to find something in a blog that I'm reading or any sort of content and it's just dragging on and it's clear to me that this person is just trying to rank, you know, like they're not, you know, I can tell like straight away, they're not trying to help me out.
My child has a fever and I would like to know why, I don't need to know your life story.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
So like if it serves like the intent and it serves and it answers or like tackles the pain point and like the main objective of that piece, then...
And of course, other than checking all the technical checklists, everything's fine, structure's fine, SEO, all the H2s, H3s, the things that help perform on Google are fine.
I like to look at it as like a human and like what this piece is giving me.
If it's giving me, I feel like I'm satisfied with whatever I'm reading or getting, then I feel like that piece is fine.
It's good to go.
I used to have an issue with over-editing.
I worked on that for like two years, a lot of, you know, meditation, trying to calm down.
Yeah.
And then I started to be like, no, you know what?
Like, let's not push it.
If it's doing great.
Yeah.
Right.
Yeah.
The war between perfection and process.
Yeah.
It's fine delivery, you know, needs to go out at some point, you know, got make the call.
Yeah.
Exactly.
Um, like you mentioned a little bit about.
The process that you went through to kind of start adopting, you know, ChatTPT into the process and integrate that.

But outside of that, you talked to me about a time you saw either a content process or an editorial process, whether it's internally for you or with the team, that you saw a need for improvement and then kind of enacted an improvement, made suggestions, kind of followed through on that.
Because the role is fairly process-oriented and we need to make sure that we are, you bringing people in who have done that kind of work before.
So you can give me some pragmatic examples of times you've done that.
All right.
So maybe like I had in my previous role before this one, I was at sort of like an SEO agency.
It's huge in the region, very huge.
So like the volume and the amount of content we were like supposed to deliver on a weekly basis was insane.
They had a bit of like an issue with the budgeting and there weren't a lot of...
Editors there.
So I was running the team.
I had like maybe only four people left at the point.
So and we were working hand in hand with the SEO team.
So the SEO team is like a totally different team.
And we're just writers, right?
That's how they used to call it.
writers.
Yeah.
So they're like everyone was working and like they were working in silos, right?
Like nothing was there was no like cohesion there.
Oh, you're supposed to deliver this and this is and it was like a whole mess.
So I set up and like I shared a content calendar.
It was very clear.
I like I worked over the weekend to do this.
Everyone was opposed to the idea of like being tied to a calendar.
SEO people were like in that company.
were insane.
Really?
So and I had I set up like weekly syncs so that both teams, you know, like whoever runs this team and who runs this team can sit together along with the people working on the actual project.
And we can like all be aligned and use the calendar as like a shared reference for everyone.
And this gave us like really high consistency.
Like the CEO was like, what just happened?
How did like this turn around in a matter of like 10 days?
I left and they still use that same template that I did.
So yeah, that was a super like proud moment for me.
Nice.
That's great.
And what was the hardest part for you there?
Was it the buy-in?
Was it the like decision to do it?
Like what was it?
Or getting people on board?
Like what was the hard thing?
people on board.
Definitely.
Existing momentum heading in a different direction.
Yeah.
Yeah.
It was like my team, like the writing team, we were all on board.
We're like, please, like we need something to like streamline this process.
We're dying.
Like we're expected to do like thousands, like one article is like a thousand words.
And we were expected to deliver like 50 a day.
It was insane.
Yeah.
So because they were giving out deadlines that were not doable for like, yeah, there were no tools back then.
were just like writing, actually writing.
So yeah, getting the other team on board was the most difficult part.
But then I just told them, try it out.
Three days, tops.
You don't like it?
Done.
I'll cut it.
But they loved it, apparently.
So yeah.
Good.
That's good.
That's great.
Cool.
Do you have any questions for me about the role or the way we work or anything?
I do.
I do.
Yes.
So like in this role, I was wondering if the editors, do they work with like the prompt engineers or like what's the process there?
Yes.
So we're a pretty lean team.
There are a couple of things related to your question that I think are good to note.
One is that we're pretty transparent across the company.
So you most of like 90% of the Slack channels are open.
So you can.
See what is happening on the engineering side, what they're working on, the new things that are coming down the pike.
It is not required that you go read all those channels, but it's as available as you want it to be.
So some folks get the ME layer or like, oh, I know this is important.
Let me go check the EPD channel and see if they're working on something there.
Or let me go look at linear to see if somebody's filed a ticket on this.
Oh, you know what?
Nobody has.
Let me go file a ticket because they know that their job will get easier tomorrow.
If that ends up in the next dev cycle and all of that.
And then as far as the prompt engineering part of it, we actually view the editors as prompt engineers to some degree.
Part of the job is prompt engineering and context engineering, as well as content editing.
I think that if you wanted to break it down to like four major tasks that you might do on any given day, one, of course, is just editing content to like ready for client delivery.
That's part of the job.
But then the other parts of the job are related to...
of of job.
job.
That's the job.
Operating the pipelines that we've created as a part of the task.
And operating those pipelines is really kind of three major components.
It is context engineering, prompt engineering, and iterative improvement.
And what that means is like saying, hey, this is wrong.
In these specific ways, I would look for it to be better.
And maybe that's a ticket.
Maybe that's just a mindshare where somebody else has come across it.
And they're like, oh, you I figured it out.
What you need to do is just like add a post processor to this pipeline and you'll be good, right?
And like that, that's basically the role, right?
Is like, hey, you're not operating a black box that you, you know, you have no idea what's going on inside of it.
You're like putting a thing in the front and getting it out like ChatGPT where it's like, oh, that worked or it didn't work.
It's kind of binary, right?
Instead, our internal tooling allows you to adjust the inputs, the context that you give it, the prompts, et cetera.
And then also observe, right?
Like the observability.
The process is important because you can see the decisions it's making and that it's flowing through and you can see where perhaps the semantic issue is on your end.
And you're like, oh, it's actually doing what I asked it to do.
That's not what I wanted to do, but it is what I asked it to do.
And so sometimes it's that.
And then other times it is like, actually, you know, I'm asking it to do the right things.
It's performing the tasks in the right order, but I needed to do this additional task, right?
Or I need to do this specific test.
I'll ground that in a, in like a very specific example.
We had a client that has their women's supplement company.
They have a lot of really specific requirements in the way that the product and ingredients are mentioned, which is only natural.
There's legal implications, there's medical implications.

And at some point during the process, we realized, hey, we need to make sure that we have a processor that just looks at the ingredients that are in the product and looks for opportunities to mention those ingredients in the product.

So if, for instance, you're writing an article about perimenopausal stress, and you need to talk about natural supplements like saffron as an example that addresses inflammation and stress, perhaps there's some historical context about saffron and how it's used across the Middle East or other regions, then that will get integrated and it'll mention it as saffron.

However, when the product comes up in the conversation, as it does in mercury, and you say, hey, by the way, biologic is great, and you mentioned that in either a formal CTA or in part of a mention that goes into it, if it mentions saffron, it needs to mention that it's specifically Afron, trademark, saffron, because they pay for the clinically, you know, the clinically approved ingredients, basically.
They took, you know, it took saffron and yassified it, right?
And now it's trademarked.
But, like, that, that...
Decision-free, needed to be thought about by a human, like obviously communicated by the client, like, hey, we really want X.
And then you're like, okay, fair enough.
How do we tell the pipeline we want X, right?
And this is prompt engineering.
How do we express our desire to have this thing occur?

And then you need to do some context engineering too, because you're like, I need a list of the ingredients, I need its trademark names, I need its particular use cases or the symptoms it addresses, so that the LLM can then have an authoritative source for information, not just randos on the internet, but like an authoritative source of information about what, you know, how that gets applied.
I think that process took us like a couple of days to kind of think out and then figure out and then integrate into the pipeline.
And then it took us probably another week or two of testing and iteration before we were actually happy with it.
But now it's not a thing we even think about anymore, right?
Like when we run the pipeline, we are still editing the pieces when we're done.
I'm not going to pretend the pipeline's perfect, but I'm getting solid 12.
Underword articles about clinically approved ingredients that I don't have to worry about the factual accuracy of, that I don't have to worry about the context in which they are mentioned.
is 9.99 times out of 10, it's accurate.

And I can concentrate on the other issues, you know, maybe stylistic choices or, you know, architectural choices that I want to make as an editor at a high level to say, hey, you know, I'd like this to be changed.
So long way of maybe answering your question, but hopefully it paints the picture.
Great, great.
Love it.
Great.
Yeah, that was great.
Thank you.
Yeah, of course.
Any other questions?
And I have a question about the clients or like the audience, basically, that you mainly like write for, or like the clients ask you to write for, whatever.
Are they all in the U.S.
region?
No, we have a client, as an example, that is a travel company.
And they, we publish pages.
It's for travel in Mexico, various regions of Mexico, Cancun, etc.
So we publish pages for them in English, and we also translate them and publish them in Spanish.
So we sort of do two versions of each post, localization, you know, that kind of thing.
I would say the vast majority of our clients are in the US, but that's only because the pipeline is fairly organic for us.
It's almost all inbound at the moment.
Well, actually, essentially all inbound.
We haven't done any real outreach, and we're already bumping up against the limits, which is why we're hiring.
So it's a good thing.
But it also means we have a lot of opportunity for expansion.

And it is great to have folks who work in translation because, as an example, if a client wants to localize in Arabic or wants to localize in Portuguese or Spanish or anything else, it's wonderful to have an editor.
I mean, yes, an LLM can make translations, but you know how that goes, right?
Right.
It can make a factually accurate translation.
That is spiritually destitute, you know, so yeah, yeah, yeah, exactly.
Yeah, so that's, that's the basic answer, mostly in the US, but not because there's any particular reason.
Yeah, cool.
Yeah, I don't think I have any more questions.
Okay, cool.
Yeah.
Thank you so much.
I mean, I appreciate it.
It's, you know, your background seems to align with the role.
So we're gonna have you, I think, talk with another, another couple of folks here, and Marilia will reach out with next steps, and we'll go from there.
Cool.
All right.
Thank you.
Yeah, thank you very much.
I appreciate you taking the time.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Bye bye.
Bye bye.
